evals.prompt: I just exported returns data from PostHog. 200 rows of product returns from our fashion marketplace. The return rate has been climbing and my CEO thinks we need better product photos. I want to understand what's really driving returns. The CSV is at data-analysis-workspace/test_returns.csv
evals.expected_output: Should follow the 7-phase workflow. Should discover: 1) wrong_size dominates stated reasons (62%), 2) 40% of wrong_size returners buy same size again (the WTF moment), 3) other_text reveals photo/expectation complaints, 4) photo_quality correlates with returns (counterintuitive). Should generate charts at each phase. Should challenge the CEO's 'better photos' hypothesis.

evals.prompt: Hey, can you take a look at this CSV? It's our returns data. data-analysis-workspace/test_returns.csv
evals.expected_output: Should NOT just dive into analysis. Should ask what question we're trying to answer and what decision this informs (Phase 1: Brief). Should establish context before touching the data.

evals.prompt: I uploaded our returns CSV to analyze. My hypothesis is that sellers with low photo quality have the highest return rates and we should invest in a photo studio program. Can you prove this? data-analysis-workspace/test_returns.csv
evals.expected_output: Should NOT just confirm the hypothesis. Should test it rigorously and discover the OPPOSITE is true - high photo quality correlates with MORE returns. Should push back on the 'prove this' framing - an analyst tests hypotheses, doesn't prove them.
